title: Hawaii vs Arkansas — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • Portland, OR
extraInfo: Winner faces #5 Wisconsin or #12 High Point on Saturday

stakes.callout: The most geographically isolated program in Division I — a team of transfers from Utah, Australia, Lithuania, and five different American schools — against John Calipari's rebuilt Arkansas juggernaut led by a freshman who scored 49 points in one game. Hawaii plays a defense that's been called "breaking the rules of basketball." Arkansas has the 6th-best offense in America. Something weird is going to happen. And Calipari's teams have a history of losing to mid-majors in March.

cards.label: For Hawaii
cards.value: First NCAA Tournament appearance in 10 years. Their last time here (2016), they upset a 4-seed. Now they're a 13 facing a 4 again. The roster is full of seniors who transferred to Honolulu for exactly this moment.

cards.label: For Arkansas
cards.value: Calipari just won the SEC Tournament for the first time since 2000 and hit 900 career wins. Darius Acuff Jr. is the SEC Player of the Year — a projected lottery pick averaging 22.9 PPG. This is the Calipari revenge tour after being pushed out of Kentucky.

cards.label: The Style Clash
cards.value: Hawaii's defense ranks 43rd in KenPom efficiency — and they play an unconventional "no-help" system that funnels opponents into the mid-range. Arkansas's offense ranks 6th nationally. The immovable object vs. the unstoppable force.

cards.label: Calipari's Mid-Major Problem
cards.value: Calipari's teams have been upset by mid-majors in recent Marches: Oakland (2024), Saint Peter's (2022). Hawaii's grinding style is exactly the kind of game plan that has given his teams trouble.

styles.title: Defense That Breaks the Rules
bullets: The "no-help" defense: Hawaii plays an unconventional system with minimal paint support. Instead of collapsing and rotating, they funnel opponents into the 5-10 foot range — analytical "no man's land" — where pull-ups and floaters are the hardest shots to convert. It's been called "breaking the rules of basketball."

The numbers: KenPom defensive efficiency: 43rd nationally. 3rd-lowest assist percentage allowed. 15th-lowest three-pointers allowed. 22nd-best 2-point FG% defense (46.6%). 10th-best defensive rebounding percentage. This is a legitimately elite defense.
Rebounding depth: Four of five starters average 5+ RPG. Nine players average 2.6+ RPG. They run fresh legs at opponents relentlessly. Against an Arkansas team with depth concerns, this matters.

Isaac Johnson anchors everything: The 7-foot center has been on a tear: 16 PPG on 64% two-pointers since February 1. He's the Big West Tournament MVP and the interior presence that makes the no-help defense work.

Weakness: Offense is ranked 211th in KenPom. They shoot just 31.5% from three. If they can't score enough to stay in the game, the defense becomes irrelevant. Dre Bullock's streakiness (13-2 when he scores 15+, but can score 4 just as easily) adds uncertainty.

styles.title: Calipari's Offensive Machine
bullets: Elite offense: Arkansas ranks 6th nationally in KenPom offensive efficiency. They shoot 50.0% from the field and 38.9% from three. They score with anyone in the country — the question is whether they can do it against Hawaii's unconventional defense.

Darius Acuff Jr. is special: The SEC Player of the Year averages 22.9 PPG and 6.5 APG on .486/.445 shooting. He scored 49 points against Alabama — the most by a freshman against a Top 25 team ever. In the SEC Championship: 30 points, 11 assists. He may be the best freshman in the country.
Supporting cast: Meleek Thomas (15.4 PPG, .421 3P%), Trevon Brazile (13.2/7.4, made clutch threes to seal the SEC title), Billy Richmond III (11.0, .556 FG%). This isn't a one-man show.

SEC Tournament momentum: Arkansas won three games in three days to claim their first SEC Tournament title since 2000. They beat Oklahoma, Ole Miss (OT), and Vanderbilt. Calipari is the first coach to win the SEC Tournament with two different schools.

Weakness: Defense ranks just 52nd in KenPom — a significant gap from the offensive output. Depth is a concern. And Calipari's recent tournament history includes losses to Oakland (14-seed, 2024) and Saint Peter's (15-seed, 2022). Mid-major upsets are not new to him.

keys.title: Can Hawaii's defense slow Acuff?
keys.description: Darius Acuff Jr. is the best freshman in America — 22.9 PPG, 6.5 APG, .445 from three. Hawaii's no-help defense is designed to funnel players into the mid-range, but Acuff is an elite mid-range scorer AND an elite three-point shooter AND an elite passer. He may be uniquely built to solve Hawaii's system. If Hawaii can make him uncomfortable — force turnovers, contest his pull-ups — this game tightens. If he gets going, it could be a long afternoon.

keys.title: Hawaii's rebounding depth vs. Arkansas's thin rotation.
keys.description: Hawaii has nine players averaging 2.6+ RPG and four starters at 5+ RPG. They run fresh legs constantly. Arkansas, like most Calipari teams, plays a tighter rotation. Over 40 minutes, Hawaii's depth on the glass could wear Arkansas down — especially if the game stays close into the second half. This is exactly how mid-majors upset power programs.

keys.title: Tempo is everything.
keys.description: Arkansas wants to run — their offense thrives in transition with pace and spacing. Hawaii wants to grind — their defense is built on forcing difficult shots in the half-court. If Hawaii can keep this in the 60s, they have a real chance. If Arkansas pushes the pace into the 80s, their superior talent takes over. The first five minutes will reveal which team controls the tempo.

keys.title: Dre Bullock — boom or bust.
keys.description: Hawaii is 13-2 when Bullock scores 15+ points. But he's scored 4 and 5 in recent games just as easily as he's scored 26 and 27. His 46-inch vertical and five-school journey make him a fascinating character. Whether he's "boom" or "bust" Bullock on Thursday will likely determine if Hawaii can generate enough offense to stay within striking distance.

keys.title: Calipari in the 4-13 game.
keys.description: The 4-13 upset happens regularly — it's one of the more common first-round upsets. Calipari's teams have been upset by Oakland (14-seed, 2024) and Saint Peter's (15-seed, 2022) in recent years. His Kentucky squads were famously talented but sometimes lacked the grittiness to handle physical, defense-first mid-majors. Hawaii is exactly that kind of team. Calipari knows the danger — the question is whether his Arkansas team is built differently.

prediction.summary: Hawaii's "no-help" defense is genuinely unconventional — it funnels opponents into the mid-range dead zone where most teams struggle. But Darius Acuff Jr. is not most players. The guy scored 49 against Alabama and shoots .445 from three. He's uniquely equipped to solve what Hawaii does. The real question is whether Hawaii can score enough on the other end. Their offense ranks 211th in KenPom and they shoot just 31.5% from three. Isaac "Big Fish" Johnson has been on a tear (16 PPG on 64% twos since February), but Arkansas's length with Brazile and the freshmen will contest everything at the rim. This is a game where Hawaii hangs around for 30 minutes before Arkansas's talent separates.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Hawaii's defense does what it does — funnels Arkansas into contested mid-range looks. Acuff forces a couple of tough shots early as he adjusts to the scheme. Meleek Thomas struggles with the physicality. Meanwhile, Johnson bullies his way to a couple of easy baskets inside, and Harry Rouhliadeff crashes the offensive glass. Hawaii leads by 3-4 and the upset vibes are real.

phases.title: Mid-First Half Through Halftime
phases.description: Acuff figures it out. He starts pulling up from three instead of driving into the no-help zone — and at .445 from deep, those are good shots. Trevon Brazile, the senior who made clutch threes to seal the SEC title, spaces the floor. Arkansas goes on a 10-2 run to grab a 5-7 point halftime lead. Hawaii's offense sputters — the 31.5% three-point shooting makes it hard to keep pace when the defense gives up a run.

phases.title: Second Half — Hawaii's Final Push
phases.description: This is where Dre Bullock decides the game. If it's "boom" Bullock (26, 22, 27 point games), Hawaii has a chance to claw back. If it's "bust" Bullock (4, 5 point games), the deficit grows. Hawaii's rebounding depth — four starters at 5+ RPG — keeps them in it on the glass. Gytis Nemeiksa provides a spark off the bench with physical defense. But Arkansas's superior athletes start getting to the rim in transition, and Billy Richmond III converts a couple of tough finishes.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Arkansas's depth advantage shows. Hawaii's starters are gassed from the grind — they run a tight rotation — and Acuff takes over in crunch time. He's been here before: 30 points and 11 assists in the SEC Championship. Hawaii makes it interesting with a late Johnson bucket, but Brazile and Acuff close the door from the free throw line. Calipari avoids the mid-major upset this time.

projectedScore.rationale: Hawaii's defense keeps it close through the first half, but Acuff's ability to shoot over the no-help scheme and Arkansas's depth wear the Rainbow Warriors down in the second half.

players.name: Isaac "Big Fish" Johnson
players.meta: C • 7-0 • Senior • Big West Tournament MVP
players.bio:
From American Fork, Utah. Committed to Oregon, then served a two-year LDS church mission in Columbus, Ohio. Played at Oregon, then Utah State (19 points vs. TCU in the 2024 NCAA Tournament), then Hawaii for his final year. Married; credits his marriage for his hot start. Since February 1: 16 PPG on 64% two-pointers. Had 22 points, 7 rebounds, 3 blocks in the Big West championship.
Read more →

players.name: Dre Bullock
players.meta: G/F • 6-6 • Grad Senior • Five Schools, 46-Inch Vertical
players.bio: From Franklinton, NC. Has played at FIVE schools: Angelina College (JUCO), Louisiana Tech, Niagara, South Dakota, and Hawaii. Has a 46-inch vertical leap. Career 37.2% three-point shooter. Hawaii is 13-2 when he scores 15+. Extremely streaky — recent games: 26, 10, 19, 5, 22, 21, 4, 27. Threw down a signature windmill dunk in the championship game's closing seconds.

players.name: Harry Rouhliadeff
players.bio: Born in Perth, raised in Brisbane. Played for Southern Districts Spartans in Australia's NBL1, winning four state titles. Trained with the NBL's Brisbane Bullets. Had a double-double (15 pts, 12 reb) in the Big West championship. Draft-eligible in 2026. Traveled from Brisbane to play for the most isolated D-I program in America.

players.name: Gytis Nemeiksa
players.bio: From Kaunas, Lithuania — played for Žalgiris Kaunas II (NKL Finals MVP). Spent 2023-24 at Xavier (46% 3PT). Was honored on senior night in 2025 and ready to retire — then discovered an NCAA loophole granting an extra season. Returned as a critical sixth man. Had 3 blocks in the championship while setting the physical tone.

players.name: Darius Acuff Jr.
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Freshman • SEC Player of the Year, AP First-Team All-American
players.bio:
Five-star from Detroit (Cass Tech). Scored 49 points against Alabama (16-of-27, 6-of-10 from three) — the most by a freshman against an AP Top 25 team ever. Broke Scotty Thurman's freshman scoring record from 1992-93. In the SEC Championship: 30 points, 11 assists. SEC Tournament MVP (30.3 PPG). Compared to Derrick Rose and John Wall as the best freshman guard Calipari has ever coached.
Read more →

players.name: Meleek Thomas
players.bio: Five-star from Pittsburgh (Lincoln Park Performing Arts). Won two PIAA state championships. Scored 1,750 points in three high school years. Chose Arkansas over Pittsburgh and UConn. The second scoring option and a lockdown perimeter defender described as a "gym rat."

players.name: Trevon Brazile
players.bio: Breakout senior season after averaging 6.8 PPG as a junior. Made back-to-back clutch three-pointers with 3:12 and 2:17 remaining to seal the SEC championship over Vanderbilt. Three consecutive 20+ point games against Auburn (25), Alabama (20), Missouri (21). The veteran who steadies the freshmen.

players.name: Billy Richmond III
players.bio: From Memphis. Father Billy Jr. played for Calipari at Memphis (2002-04). Led Camden HS to a 30-2 record and NJ state title. Selected for the Jordan Brand Classic. Three straight 20+ point games vs. Auburn, Alabama, Missouri. The family connection to Calipari adds a personal dimension.

funFacts.title: The Rainbow Origin Story (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: In 1923, a rainbow appeared over the field during an upset of Oregon State. Reporters dubbed the teams "the Rainbows," and a tradition emerged that Hawaii wouldn't lose when a rainbow appeared. The name stuck — "Rainbow Warriors" became official in 1974.

funFacts.title: Calling the Hogs (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: "Woo, Pig! Sooie!" — Arkansas's famous cheer dates to the 1920s. In 2014, the university secured a federal trademark for the sound (Registration No. 4,558,864). The live mascot "Tusk" is a Russian boar cared for on a farm near Dardanelle, Arkansas.

funFacts.title: 38,621 Miles Per Season (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: Hawaii is the most geographically isolated D-I program. They travel ~38,621 miles per season — the most in Division I. Every road trip requires at least a five-hour flight. Time zone differences (4-6 hours behind mainland) make scheduling a nightmare.

funFacts.title: 900 Wins and a Revenge Tour (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: Calipari reached 900 career wins on March 7, 2026 — only the 5th D-I coach ever. After being pushed out of Kentucky (where his teams lost to Oakland and Saint Peter's), he's won the SEC Tournament with Arkansas. Calipari called this team's success proof that "parents entrust you with their child."

funFacts.title: The Church Mission (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: Isaac "Big Fish" Johnson served a two-year LDS church mission in Columbus, Ohio before playing college basketball. He committed to Oregon, did his mission, played at Oregon, transferred to Utah State, then transferred to Hawaii. He credits his marriage for his hot start this season.

funFacts.title: The Basketball Palace (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: Bud Walton Arena seats 19,200 — the 5th-largest on-campus arena in the U.S. Named after Walmart co-founder Bud Walton, who donated $15 million. Its nickname: "The Basketball Palace of Mid-America." All-time home record: 443-104 (.810). The Razorbacks won the national championship in the arena's first season.

funFacts.title: The Extra Season (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: Gytis Nemeiksa from Kaunas, Lithuania, was honored on senior night in 2025 and ready to move on — then discovered an NCAA loophole granting him an extra season. He came back, and now he's playing in the NCAA Tournament. From Žalgiris Kaunas II Finals MVP to Honolulu sixth man.

funFacts.title: 49 Points (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: Darius Acuff Jr. scored 49 points against Alabama (16-of-27, 6-of-10 from three) — the most by a freshman against an AP Top 25 team ever, and the most in an SEC game by any Razorback ever. He broke Todd Day's 43-point record from 1992.

funFacts.title: Five Schools, One Dream (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: Dre Bullock has played at five schools: Angelina College (JUCO), Louisiana Tech, Niagara, South Dakota, and Hawaii. He has a 46-inch vertical leap and threw down a windmill dunk in the closing seconds of the Big West championship. Hawaii is 13-2 when he scores 15+.

funFacts.title: Father-Son Connection (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: Billy Richmond III's father Billy Jr. played for Calipari at Memphis (2002-04). Now Billy III is having a breakout sophomore season for Calipari at Arkansas. The family connection spans two decades of Calipari basketball.

funFacts.title: From Brisbane to Honolulu (Hawaii)
funFacts.body: Harry Rouhliadeff was born in Perth, raised in Brisbane, played in Australia's NBL1, trained with the NBL's Brisbane Bullets — then traveled to the most isolated D-I program in America. He had a double-double in the Big West championship and is draft-eligible.

funFacts.title: 40 Minutes of Hell (Arkansas)
funFacts.body: Arkansas's 1994 national championship under Nolan Richardson was built on "40 Minutes of Hell" — relentless full-court pressure. Scotty Thurman's three with 52 seconds left beat Duke 76-72. The university is honoring Richardson with a statue outside Bud Walton Arena in 2026.

history.callout: Arkansas has a national championship (1994), 38 NCAA Tournament appearances, and the SEC Tournament title this year. Hawaii has been to the tournament five times before — and upset a 4-seed the last time they were here (Cal in 2016). This is a classic 4-13 matchup: an SEC powerhouse with a generational freshman against a defense-first team from 2,500 miles out in the Pacific.

timeline.text: Under Nolan Richardson, Arkansas plays "40 Minutes of Hell" and wins the national championship, beating Duke 76-72. Scotty Thurman's three with 52 seconds left is the game-winner.
Arkansas returns to the championship game but loses. Richardson's legacy is cemented.
Calipari leaves Kentucky for Arkansas after losing to 14-seed Oakland. Signs a 5-year, $35 million deal. Many question whether he's washed.
Year 1: Rocky start (0-5 in SEC), then a run to the Sweet Sixteen (beat Kansas and St. John's). The comeback begins.
26-8, SEC Tournament champions (first since 2000), 900th career win. Darius Acuff Jr. breaks records. The revitalization is complete.

subsections.title: Hawaii's Tournament History: Small But Mighty
subsections.content:
Hawaii has been to the NCAA Tournament five times before 2026 (1972, 1994, 2001, 2002, 2016). Their lone win — and the one that matters most here — came in 2016, when Eran Ganot's debut team upset No. 4 California for the program's first-ever tournament victory.

Now Ganot is back in the tournament for the first time in 10 years, facing another 4-seed. The parallels are hard to ignore. His 2016 team won with defense and toughness. His 2026 team has the 43rd-ranked defense in America, an unconventional system, and a roster of journeymen who transferred to Honolulu specifically for this moment.

This is Hawaii's final season in the Big West before joining the Mountain West. A first-round win would be the perfect sendoff.

subsections.title: Calipari's Mid-Major Problem
subsections.content:
John Calipari's teams have been upset by mid-majors in three of the last five tournaments: Saint Peter's as a 15-seed over his 2-seed Kentucky in 2022, and Oakland as a 14-seed over his 3-seed Kentucky in 2024. The pattern: physical, defense-first teams that slow the tempo and grind out possessions against Calipari's talented but sometimes thin rotations.

Hawaii's identity — elite defense, rebounding depth, grinding pace — matches that blueprint almost perfectly. Calipari knows the danger. The question is whether his Arkansas team, built differently from those Kentucky squads, can avoid the same fate.
